full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Welcome to the second Sunday of our new season at the wilderness.<|7.82|> <|7.82|> The dance continues, four weeks in which we are praying with Andre Rublev's icon, the<|14.28|> <|14.28|> Trinity, and seek to embody the Nicene Creed, one of the great summarizations of Christian<|21.84|> <|21.84|> faith.<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you to Aaron Nyquist and the people of Good Shepherd New York for inspiring this<|6.18|> <|6.18|> series.<|12.24|> <|12.24|> And during this time, instead of a conventional sermon, the clergy will share over the next<|18.06|> <|18.06|> two weeks very short reflections on the Trinity.<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> followed by open space, at which time you are invited to wander the cathedral, becoming<|6.30|> <|6.30|> conscious that you are already loved and held by God, the Holy Trinity.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We begin our reflection in the first line of the Creed.<|6.44|> <|7.32|> We believe in God, the Father, the Almighty.<|13.02|> <|14.94|> As we sink into God, the Holy Trinity,<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is important to remember that belief or trust is not merely mental assent, but it<|12.52|> <|12.52|> is a choice to trust.<|16.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Rowan Williams, the Welsh poet and former archbishop, has said that the creed is a series<|7.34|> <|7.34|> of statements about where I find the anchorage for my life, where I find solid ground, home,<|18.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So when we say we believe in one God, we are saying that God is our anchor, our confidence, our refuge, our home.<|18.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And how many of you need that refuge, that anchor, that home amidst the frenetic, chaotic times in which we live?<|13.82|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For us in the Anglican tradition, this confidence, trust, refuge, and home find their expression through us, but have their genesis in God.<|17.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Before we trust God, God trusts us.<|9.40|> <|9.40|> Before we are confident in God, God is confident in us.<|17.96|> <|17.96|> Before we return home to God, God makes God's home in us.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And now to the language of Father.<|6.62|> <|6.62|> Father.<|8.58|> <|8.58|> When we refer to God as the Father, the Almighty, are we saying God is a literal man above us<|18.04|> <|18.04|> in heaven?<|19.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If we are, I'm out of here.<|2.90|> <|5.86|> All of our language for and about God is an approximation.<|11.42|> <|13.48|> The language gets us to the edge of where God dwells.<|20.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> No string of words or series of statements adequately articulates the ineffability of<|8.18|> <|8.18|> the divine.<|11.72|> <|11.72|> Instead of literalizing this imagery, the 20th century writer Mary Ann Mix says the following:<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For our purposes, the important thing to notice is the invitation that title offers us.<|10.80|> <|11.92|> Come closer.<|13.10|> <|15.02|> Come closer.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> answer.<|1.30|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Those two words, "come closer," may very well be the invitation from God this evening<|10.36|> <|10.36|> for you, the good God who is overflowing with goodness towards you, towards us, and the whole<|19.66|> <|19.66|> cosmos.<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The God who uses almighty power to fill the world with good life.<|8.64|> <|8.64|> And as we conclude our reflection,<|13.40|> <|13.40|> we can't not address the events of yesterday and<|20.12|> <|20.12|> the assassination attempt on a former president.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And our discussions this morning in the sermons, and this is me going completely off script,<|6.64|> <|6.64|> you never want me going off script, ever.<|11.68|> <|11.68|> So I'll keep this very short.<|16.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The events of yesterday and every iteration of political violence, and we've seen it here<|6.88|> <|6.88|> and there.<|7.88|> <|7.88|> You know, Gabby Giffords, the congresswoman from Arizona who was shot in 2011.<|13.88|> <|13.88|> I'll never forget where I was when that happened.<|17.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There have been a number of those over the years, actually really too many to name.<|7.62|> <|7.62|> For those of you who are close to my parents' age and my grandparents' age, you remember<|14.44|> <|14.44|> the 60s and the number of political assassinations that happened in that decade alone.<|23.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Obviously, and our presiding bishop has spoken about this in a statement earlier today, violence,<|9.82|> <|9.82|> it's too pithy to say it's never the answer.<|12.48|> <|12.48|> That's a little too pithy.<|15.56|> <|15.56|> Violence is a failure of imagination.<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And in order for us to have a way forward that is hopeful, that is wise, that is gentle,<|9.82|> <|9.82|> that is generative, that allows us to hear God saying, "Come closer."<|19.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We need that imagination, that faith, that hope that the other means well, even at their<|15.76|> <|15.76|> worst.<|17.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and that we mean well even at our worst.<|5.36|> <|5.36|> One of the purposes of political violence is to make us suspicious of one another,<|13.88|> <|13.88|> to ask who's in and who's out.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we come to altars like this one and we burn incense and we listen to music and we<|7.82|> <|7.82|> pray and we're in community with each other because we love each other.<|16.40|> <|16.40|> We're not suspicious of each other.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we assume that the other means well, even at their worst.<|8.06|> <|10.22|> So tonight as you pray during open space, I invite you to hear that beautiful and compelling invitation from a little bit earlier.<|22.94|> <|22.94|> Amen.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How is God inviting you to come closer?<|5.00|> <|6.88|> And this is a sermon for a different time.<|8.90|> <|9.84|> Come closer, again, that's an approximation.<|12.86|> <|12.86|> You're already in union with God.<|16.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What things do you need to let go of?<|4.46|> <|4.46|> By extension, what things do we as a society and a community need to let go of to experience<|13.14|> <|13.14|> the union we already have with God and with one another?<|19.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And how is violence a weight, a barrier to that union?<|11.48|> <|15.44|> We'll sing and then we'll pray.<|19.16|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that invitation to prayer, to relationship, to community with God, the Holy Trinity, is always yours. Amen.<|12.44|><|endoftext|>
